prompt: Set up a FieldOps Kitchen & Training Console that enables service teams to execute standardized on-site work orders while reinforcing technician knowledge through guided cooking-style workflows and measurable learning. Regular Technicians sign in on a Yew-based web UI (optimized for tablet use) to open their assigned job, see the current business state (Scheduled, En Route, On Site, In Progress, Waiting on Parts, Completed, Canceled), and follow a step-by-step "recipe" that supports multiple concurrent timers (for example, a 15-minute refrigerant stabilization timer plus a 5-minute safety hold) with audible and visual reminders. Each step can be marked complete, paused, and resumed later without losing timers or notes, and configurable tip cards (admin-authored) can be pinned to specific steps such as "HOA access checklist" or "EPA recovery reminder." A local map-style view displays the job location and the technician's own recorded trajectory for the visit; the UI enforces arrival and departure check-ins and allows an optional privacy mode that reduces stored location precision and hides the trail from non-admin users. Supervisors view learning analytics and trend reports that roll up completion rate, quiz accuracy, time spent, and review count across knowledge points and learning units tied to each workflow; reports can be filtered by date range (MM/DD/YYYY), branch, and role, exported to CSV, and restricted so technicians only see their own results while supervisors see their team and administrators see all. The backend uses Actix-web to expose offline-first REST-style APIs that the Yew frontend calls over the local network or on the same machine, with PostgreSQL providing durable persistence, auditing, and reporting. Work orders are implemented as a generic state machine with allowed transitions, required fields per transition, automatic routing rules (for example, dispatch to the on-call queue when priority is High and SLA is under 4 hours), SLA timers, and timeout alerts shown in-app; every transition and user action writes an immutable processing log to support closed-loop tracking. Incremental update and consistency are handled by a scheduled sync job that runs every 10 minutes between local replicas, using change detection fingerprints (ETag-style hashes), soft deletes with a 90-day retention window, and historical version retention for 30 versions per record; conflicts are resolved by a deterministic merge policy that prioritizes later timestamps but never overwrites completed state logs, forcing a supervisor review when both sides edited the same step notes. Address normalization and geocoding rely on an offline ZIP+4 and street index bundled with the installation, and service-radius validation checks that the job is within a configurable limit (default 30 miles) from the assigned branch. The notification center is strictly in-app only in offline mode, supporting templated event messages (signup success, schedule change, cancellation, review result), delivery and read receipts, retry with exponential backoff up to 5 attempts, unsubscribe controls, and rate limiting to 20 notifications per user per hour. Authentication is local username/password with salted hashing; sensitive fields such as home addresses are encrypted at rest, location trails can be masked by role, and exports are watermarked with the requesting user and timestamp to reduce unauthorized redistribution.
